items.q: What is the main advantage of asymmetric encryption?
items.correct: Secure key exchange without sharing a secret in advance.
items.misconception: It is faster than symmetric encryption.
items.why: Students think asymmetric is just 'better'. It's actually SLOWER than symmetric. Its advantage is solving key distribution - the public key can be shared openly without compromising security.

items.q: What is the difference between an IP address and a MAC address?
items.correct: MAC is a hardware identifier (fixed); IP is a logical network address (assignable).
items.misconception: They are the same thing with different names.
items.random_wrong: IP is for input, MAC is for output.
items.why: Students treat these as synonyms. MAC is burned into the network card (Layer 2); IP is assigned by the network (Layer 3). Both are needed to route packets to a device.

items.q: What happens when 11111111 + 1 is computed in an 8-bit register?
items.correct: Overflow - the result requires 9 bits but only 8 are available.
items.why: Students miss that registers have fixed width. The mathematical result is 9 bits, but the 8-bit register drops the carry-out, producing 00000000 with an overflow flag.

items.q: Von Neumann's EDVAC report describing stored-program computers dates from:
items.why: It's 1945. “1936” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Ken Thompson and Dennis Ritchie's UNIX was first released in:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1969. “1965” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Year Von Neumann's EDVAC report was circulated?
items.why: The real figure is 1945 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
